speaker: interviewer
text: Date, August 14, 1996. Survivor, Claire Boren, maiden name, Goldbarten-Langer. Interviewer, Susan Douglass. City/state, Rumson, New Jersey. Country, USA. The language is in English. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Susan Douglass, S-U-S-A-N D-O-U-G-L-A-S-S. Today I am conducting an interview with Claire Boren in Rumson, New Jersey. The interview is on August 14, 1996. The interview will be in English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please state your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Claire Boren, B-O-R-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Claire, C-L-A-I-R-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Please state your name at birth and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Chaia Langer, L-A-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Were you ever known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was known also as Clara Goldbarten, G-O-L-D-B-A-R-T-E-N. My mother remarried, and that's my stepfather's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born July 8, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't added it up. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the small village of Mizoch, which is in the Ukraine. It used to be Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Would you please spell the name of the village you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-Z-O-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Please say and spell the names of your parents. Let's start with your biological father.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Yosef Langer, L-A-N-G-E-R. And my mother was Ann Langer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mother remarried. What was the full name of your stepfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emil Goldbarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And would you spell Goldbarten for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-O-L-D-B-A-R-T-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How many other children were there in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my immediate family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister Bina, B-I-N-A. She was born after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what year was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did your father do to earn a living before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the family business. It was really-- my grandmother, and he, and I think one other uncle or aunt were in the tanning business. They would process the hides of animals, make it into leather, and then sell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What do you remember most about your home in Mizoch?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have, you know, really nice memories of the home. I have vivid memories of the house, of the bedroom that I guess was my parents' bedroom that I used to play in. There was a dressing table there with a mirror and two cabinets. I used to play in there. I played a lot in the yard. I remember I had a dog, a shepherd--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rex.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to this dog during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was that the dog disappeared. And then afterwards, I realized that the Germans had just taken him away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get back to your home. How many rooms did the house have?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the front of this yard, there was a house where my grandmother lived with two bachelor uncles. And in back of that was a longish house. And it was split. And my mother and father and I lived in one part, and one of his sisters, Tamma, her husband, and two children lived in the other part of the house. So there were a number of rooms. I don't remember exactly how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did this house have electricity--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have indoor plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did anyone in the village have a phone that you recall hearing about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How would you describe the religious life of your family as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm really, you know, not aware that much. But I would imagine it was-- we were traditionally religious. I know that my mother has said that every Friday night, everybody went to my grandmother's house for dinner, you know? That she really didn't make the Sabbath meal. We just ate together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you were very young when the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- you lived in the area that was occupied by the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --rather than the Germans. So you really weren't-- your family wasn't affected by the German occupation until after June of 1941. Do you have recollections of any changes going on in that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometime-- I guess it was in '42-- must have been-- that we-- we went to the ghetto. I don't remember the actual change. I certainly remember the difference-- the difference being that we were crowded in, many families in one house. My mother, father, and I just had one room. And I remember my mother used to suffer a lot from migraine headaches. I remember standing near her bed and being afraid to walk or talk, and just being very silent.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I just remember a lot of people being there, just being very crowded-- not, at that point, that particularly unhappy because there were, like, a lot of children around. But just crowded, a sense of fear. Certainly not-- there wasn't enough food. You remember shortages of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shortages of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being limited in your freedom of movement while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I was allowed to leave the house by myself, whereas in the old house, I remember just being out in the yard with other children, with my dog, just, you know, running around-- whereas here, I think I was just in the house and that was it. I was not allowed out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were other members of your family in the same area or house with you in the ghetto, that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't know. I would imagine they were, because I know there were a lot of people there. I remember babies and children, just a lot of people. So I would think that some of them were other members of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your grandmother with you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is your most vivid memory of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: My most vivid memory is standing by a door that had a curtain in it-- it was a glass door-- and pushing aside that curtain. And I guess I knew that I wasn't supposed to be looking out because someone must have told me to. And there was this public hanging. There were several people being hung there. And they had placards across their chest. And afterwards, I realized-- or at some point, someone explained to me that these were-- Jews had been caught smuggling food into the ghetto. But I remember them hanging there with this big sign across the chest. And I just stood there, watching this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember anyone pulling you away from the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just remember standing there, being horrified, and feeling guilty because I knew I wasn't supposed to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were in the ghetto, your father had set up some plans for your future safety. What were the plans that he had set up for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had arranged to have, I think, many members of our family to go into hiding with non-Jewish families. And he paid them. And the story I remember was that this particular man that-- he was a Volksdeutsch. He was supposed to keep us for the duration of the war. And he came to the house one day and told my father that we had to go. And my mother didn't want to go. She didn't want to leave. I guess she just didn't want to believe that we had to leave. And he kept insisting that we go that day. And for whatever reason, my father wasn't ready to leave. Maybe he was waiting for other members-- to arrange for the other members to leave. But my mother, and I, and he were walking. And we were either in a field, or a cemetery, or something like a big, open space. And my mother was very upset with him. So I think she didn't even talk to him because he wasn't coming with us or he was making her go. And I said goodbye to my father. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been October of '42--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because that's when the ghetto was liquidated. We went to this man to-- who was gonna hide us. And we learned that the very next day, the ghetto was liquidated. So he must have known, and that's why he came and insisted that we leave that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you got to his farm, where on the farm were you hid?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some sort of false wall and some sort of a hiding space that we were hidden in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you allowed to walk around in the evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't have very strong memories of this place. I know that this man who was supposed to keep us for the duration did not keep us for very long. My mother has told me it wasn't very long. I don't have such strong feelings of this place. But the next place that we went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I remember we were walking along a road, and I was very tired. And I remember sitting down and saying, I'm not going anymore. The-- you know, I just-- I want to walk. And my mother picked out this house, this farmhouse, and apparently knocked on the door. And they took us in. And they were religious people. They were some sort of evangelical sect. And they took us in. They were very poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They hid us in the barn, where they dug out a hole, on top of which a pig stood. The hole was long enough that my mother and I could lie down, just, you know-- like, if one of us was in the back, the other one had to be on the side. We could not stand up. I think we could sit up. And at night, they would let us come out. It was totally pitch black. It was like a grave. And at the beginning, my mother would tell me stories. You know, she afterwards told me she told me every book she ever read, every movie she ever read. But after a while, I guess she started sort of running out of stories. Whatever the reason was, they also lowered food to us once a day.

speaker: interviewee
text: My days and nights got mixed up. So when she was awake, [POINTING] I was asleep. And when I was awake, she was asleep. So I retreated into this total fantasy world, you know? I just lived in this make-believe world. And it got to the point where I lost all touch with reality. I just-- I just left this world. Do you recall anything about this fantasy world at this point in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it started out where I was thinking mostly about my father. You know, I was gonna find him. He was gonna come and get me out of here. It was that kind of thinking. And after a while-- I don't know what I was thinking of-- but it was-- I was out of it. And we were there a few months. And it got to the point where the-- this-- my mother tells me this, that the people-- these peasants said to her that the devil possessed me; she should kill me or something. But she decided that she just had to get me out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I wasn't eating and I-- And-- and I was in just-- I didn't know where I was. So we left. And we went to some village. It was all within the same area-- to a village where I think my mother ran into someone who'd been a former maid of ours. And they kept us. And we were up in an attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but the best part of it was that I was just let loose. They just let me run with the kids, with the farm children, you know? The peasant children. And, you know, I went with them to the fields to take care of the animals and just run around. And I just came back to myself. Did you realize, as you were playing with these village children, that to admit that you were Jewish would be a dangerous thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't even know, because I think I was so out of it that I just-- I don't think I knew what was going on with me at that particular point. I was just-- as I said, I was just living in this fantasy world, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So playing with these children was a good form of therapy for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I could speak. I hadn't spoken to anybody in-- in months, because my mother was sleeping, I was awake. I hadn't, you know-- I hadn't talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how long did you stay with this former servant, where you had the time to-- the opportunity to play with the children--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --like you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a very long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from that location?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere along the way, my mother heard that an uncle of mine was with that original man, this Meltzer. So we went back to him. And there was the husband-- Arie-- of my father's sister, the one who had lived in-- in the house with us, who shared the house. He was there with his two children, a girl and the boy, and that-- one of the bachelor uncles, Leppe. And we stayed there for a while, all of us. And then we went into the woods, because we heard that there were Jews in the woods and he wasn't gonna keep us. He started talking in terms that the police were coming around, that other people-- that it was dangerous for him. So we left and went into the woods. And I think we stayed in the woods from that spring up to the following winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And from the woods, where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: And in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the woods-- I have to go back to that part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we were just living off the land. Sometimes I think about it, you know? I try to realize how did we live. But we just ate berries and other things. And I think during the night, some of the adults would go into the farms and forage for vegetables. We were always-- we were still cold and hungry. And one-- one of my memories is the fact that one time, it rained very, very hard. And I was sleeping. And I must've rolled down the hill-- there-- we were, like, on some sort of a hill-- because I woke up and I was all by myself. And then I woke up and I realized that-- you know, I don't know. My mother must have found me. But we were just living in the woods. During this time, I-- I suffered from dysentery. I also had these horrible scabs and wounds on my knees, so that there was a period when I couldn't walk, and my mother had to carry me everywhere on her back. I had these terrible scars and wounds on my knees. And also, the Ukrainians would come into the forest. The German army did not come into the forest, but the Ukrainians came in and would raid these groups of people. We were not with the partisans, because partisans wouldn't take children. But there were groups of partisans and there were groups of Jews in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever a time when you felt that your life was-- and your family's life was in danger when the Ukrainians came into the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. During one of the raids, they killed both my uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you and your mother when this occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess we were in hiding. But another one of those really vivid memories that I have is another time, when these Ukrainians came-- and for whatever reason, I was sitting, like, under a bush. I think part of it was because I couldn't walk. And they were questioning my mother. And they were talking to some of the other people. Later, she told me that she-- that they said that they were communist partisans and they were there to help us. But she recognized one of the people as being Ukrainian. So she wanted to get away. So she somehow told them there were other people and that she wanted to go get them and come back. But I remember sitting under the bush and knowing that this was dangerous. And it's one of those, like, pictures that you get in your head. And it also occurred when-- I mentioned to you Judith Kastenberg had interviewed me. And as a child-- for her to jar my memory, she-- she said, look below. And what I remembered of the Germans were the boots. And what I remembered here in this forest were the boots-- you know, those black boots. And I knew that we were in danger.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was sitting under this bush and saying to myself, I'm just gonna crawl away. And nobody is going to know I'm here. And at the same time-- it's amazing to me now, but I remember the feelings, and at the same time saying to myself, how can I crawl away and leave my mother? But the idea was that I had to crawl away and hide myself. I mean, I knew that this was dangerous. Would you identify who Judith Kastenberg is?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has done a-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] She has done a lot of studies on child survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you-- when were you interviewed by her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was interviewed by her-- I think it was in the '80s. And that's when this memory of the boots came back to me and the memory of the dog, because she started talking to me about the dog. And I had all sorts of memories come back to me of this dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm just gonna ask you one more point about Judith Kastenberg. Would you just spell her last name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's K-A-S-T-E-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you-- have you had any contact with Judith since that interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I did for a while, and I-- I did some interviews for her. But I haven't in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reconnect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at any rate, the interviews with her brought back many memories that you-- you didn't even realize.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Of some of these memories. That was one of those things of just looking down at the ground and seeing those boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember leaving the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember that we left the forest. And I remember my mother carrying me on her back because, as I said, my knees were very bad. And I remember just always being cold, and wet, and having diarrhea, and-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the forest, we went to this village. And we were hidden by a Czech man. And that was, like, the best hiding place we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it the best?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was very nice. And he-- we were in the attic. He brought us, you know, whatever food he had. I mean, it was decent food. It was bread and other things. I think it was actually bread. I remember sometimes pork. And I think that was, like, the first time I ever ate pork. And he also had a son that was my age. So while he couldn't come up to play with me, we would sort of have signals of playing through the window of this attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you deal with the clothes for yourself as a growing child?

speaker: interviewee
text: With clothes?

speaker: interviewer
text: With clothes when you were at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --last--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what happened with clothes. I remember having-- it sounds awful, but in the forest, always having lice, you know? [LAUGHTER] Sitting there, trying to get rid of the lice-- but I don't know what I did for clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you were in hiding, did your mother ever try to go back to your original home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When we were in hiding with this Czech man who kept us for most of the winter, there was some point where he became uneasy, and he felt that he couldn't hide us anymore. And somewhere along that-- the line or at-- around this point, my mother decided to go back to Mizoch. And in-- in the backyard, somewhere where our house had been, my father had hidden some money and some valuables. And she decided that if she went back there and got some of these, maybe she could pay someone to keep us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he apparently drew a map for her or whatever. But we went back to the village. And-- So she took you with her when she went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because we left--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --this Czech man. And she hid me somewhere. I thought it was like a shed of some sort. She says it was a barn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she went out to get-- to dig this money up. And that was, again, one of those horrifying nights for me that I remember. It was-- I was covered with a blanket in this dark, miserable place. And there were rats and mice all over. And I think it must have been already towards the end of the war or whatever-- I guess the end of '43, somewhere along there, or maybe the beginning of '44, because there was a lot of bombardment going on. I was, like, hiding there.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, it seemed like it was all night. I don't know if it was all night. It could have been just an hour or two. But I just remember, you know, the bombs, and-- and the artillery, and flashes, and just being hidden under this blanket with the rats and-- all over, and just really not knowing if my mother was gonna get back. You know, what if she was caught? And just thinking over and over, what will I do if she doesn't come back? Where am I going to go? What should I do? Will I remember how to go back to this other place? And just all night, going over and over, and just thinking of what will happen if she doesn't come back. Do you remember your feelings when she does come back to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was just her. I mean, she was my whole security. That was it. But sometimes when I think that at that point-- I was, like, maybe six years old-- and, you know, when I had my own children, I thought about it. I said, how can a six-year-old think in terms of trying to make a plan to run away, to actually be able to go somewhere and hide and do all of that? I-- I just can't imagine. But I remember thinking that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother dig up in the backyard of your former home?

speaker: interviewee
text: She dug up some money. I guess it was gold coins. They call it 5's. I don't know what it means. It's a fifth or something. It's a 5-- like a $5. This is a 5-something gold coin. And really, what's wonderful for me is that I have two mementos from that. One is a cigarette case that my mother gave to my father when they became engaged. And the other is a pair of earrings that belonged to my grandmother, my father's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after your mother returned to the barn with what she had dug up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to that same Czech man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his reaction when he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --saw you return?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was concerned because there were, like-- the Ukrainians or the Poles or everybody was all over. And I think we hid out in some straw. My mother said that she-- we hid out in some hay or somewhere. We didn't go back to the attic. But she would give him money for us that-- she would give him money so he could buy food. And-- and that's where we remained for a while, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. OK. I'll--
